medications.indication_rationale: Topical salicylic acid is first-line self-care for common warts, especially combined with mechanical paring/filing. Apply strictly to the lesions (protecting surrounding skin) nightly under occlusion, after soaking and filing down the dead surface. Available over the counter, so the AI Doctor can advise it directly and a pharmacist can supply it — no urgent prescription or in-person visit is needed. Treatment is prolonged (typically 4-12 weeks of daily use) and requires adherence.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply salicylic acid 17% liquid to the lesions once nightly under occlusion (tape); first soak the wart in warm water ~5 minutes and file the dead surface with a dedicated emery board. Continue daily for weeks (commonly 4-12 weeks) until resolved

medications.indication_rationale: Cryotherapy is an appropriate GP-administered second-line option for warts that are refractory to topical keratolytic. It is a routine in-clinic procedure, not an urgent one — reserved for lesions that fail to respond, per the source note's plan of GP review in ~2 weeks.

There is no bacterial infection: no surrounding warmth, spreading redness, pus, streaking or fever. Warts are viral and spread by autoinoculation, not by bacteria. Prescribing antibiotics here is an error of commission — reserve them only if genuine secondary cellulitis/lymphangitis develops, which is the safety-net trigger.
Systemic antivirals have no role in cutaneous HPV warts — aciclovir targets herpesviruses, not HPV. Prescribing one would be ineffective and inappropriate.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop picking, biting and tweezing the warts immediately — this is the single most important step to halt the spread.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Picking releases HPV from the rough surface into micro-cuts on adjacent skin (autoinoculation), which is directly causing the rapid periungual and multi-digit spread. Stopping it lets treatment work and prevents new lesions.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Every night, soak the warts in warm water for ~5 minutes, then file away the softened dead white skin with a dedicated emery board before applying the salicylic acid; wash hands thoroughly after treating.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Filing debulks the hyperkeratotic surface so the keratolytic penetrates, and hand-washing after contact reduces autoinoculation and spread to others.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not share towels, nail clippers, emery boards or files with anyone else, and keep the treated hand's tools separate.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: HPV is transmissible by direct and fomite contact; not sharing personal items limits spread to household members and to other body sites.

patient_education_points.point: These are common viral warts (caused by a skin virus, HPV) — they are benign, not a cancer, and the black dots are tiny clotted blood vessels, a normal feature of a wart rather than a danger sign.

The virus makes the skin grow a thick horny 'armour'; the acid and filing thin and destroy the infected layers over time. Treatment is often slow — commonly 4 to 12 weeks of daily treatment — and works only with consistent daily use.
The rapid spread is from picking at them, not from anything sinister — stopping picking and washing your hands after treating them is the key to getting on top of it.
The main things to watch for are covered in the safety-net advice: a lesion that starts bleeding on its own, turns dark, or grows fast, or the skin around them becoming hot, red, swollen or streaky.

follow_up_plan.interval: routine GP review in about 2 weeks if the lesions are not improving with topical treatment, to consider cryotherapy
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: response of the warts to salicylic acid and filing, whether cryotherapy is warranted for refractory lesions, adherence and technique, and reconfirmation that no lesion has developed atypical/malignant or secondarily-infected features
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: a lesion bleeding spontaneously, turning dark/irregular, ulcerating or growing rapidly; or surrounding skin becoming hot, red, swollen, or developing red streaks up the arm

must_include_items: Recognise these as benign common viral warts (verruca vulgaris) manageable with self-care, and reassure the patient that the picture is not a skin cancer
Recommend a topical keratolytic (salicylic acid) combined with soaking and filing the dead surface, applied daily, and set the expectation that it takes weeks
Instruct the patient to STOP picking/biting/tweezing the warts to halt autoinoculation, and to avoid sharing towels/nail tools
Safety-net explicitly for malignant change (spontaneous bleeding, darkening, ulceration, rapid growth) and for secondary infection (spreading redness, warmth, swelling, pus, red streaks, fever)
Arrange routine GP follow-up in ~2 weeks for cryotherapy if refractory — not urgent/emergency care
acceptable_alternatives: Pharmacist-led OTC management of the warts with the same self-care and safety-netting is an acceptable disposition
Referral for cryotherapy at routine GP follow-up as first escalation of therapy
Watchful waiting with reassurance is acceptable given many warts self-resolve, provided safety-netting is given
errors_of_omission: Failing to reassure and address the patient's cancer fear proportionately
Failing to instruct stopping picking/biting (the autoinoculation driver)
Failing to safety-net for malignant change or secondary infection
Not setting the expectation that wart treatment is prolonged (weeks)
errors_of_commission: Escalating a benign, afebrile, non-cellulitic wart presentation to an urgent same-day in-person visit, ED, or ambulance (over-triage) — replicating the source note's urgent home-visit framing
Arranging urgent biopsy or urgent dermatology to 'rule out cancer' for a clinically characteristic multiple-wart picture
Prescribing systemic antibiotics with no signs of bacterial infection
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires reassurance + topical keratolytic/filing self-care + a clear stop-picking instruction + an explicit malignancy and secondary-infection safety-net, delivered as calm self-care with routine (not urgent) follow-up. Over-triage to urgent in-person/ED or reflex antibiotics/antivirals is a scored error of commission, not caution.
